name: Physical Quantities
description: Things you can measure in physics—length, time, mass, speed, force, energy.

content.overview: A physical quantity is anything you can measure in physics. Length, time, temperature, force—all physical quantities. To measure something, you need a NUMBER and a UNIT. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
So what's a physical quantity? It's something measurable. "I'm tall" isn't a physical quantity. "I'm 180 cm tall"—that's a physical quantity. You have a number (180) and a unit (cm).

Every physical quantity needs both:
- **Number**: How much?
- **Unit**: In what measurement?

For example:
- Length: 5 meters (number = 5, unit = meters)
- Mass: 2 kilograms (number = 2, unit = kg)
- Time: 10 seconds (number = 10, unit = s)
- Speed: 60 kilometers per hour (number = 60, unit = km/h) {{viz:chart-01}}

There are two types of physical quantities:

**Scalars** - Just a number. Like mass (10 kg) or temperature (25°C). You don't care about direction.

**Vectors** - Number AND direction. Like velocity (10 m/s north) or force (50 N upward). Direction matters! {{viz:diagram-02}}

Why does this matter? Because "10 m/s" is different from "10 m/s east." "100 N" means something different than "100 N downward." The direction changes everything. {{viz:anim-01}}
keyPoints: A physical quantity = a number + a unit
Always include the unit (meters, seconds, kg, etc.)
Scalars have just a number (mass, temperature, time)
Vectors have a number and a direction (velocity, force, acceleration)
content.whyItMatters: Physics is about measuring and predicting. You can't predict or calculate if you don't know what quantities matter and how to measure them. Physical quantities are the language of physics.
